content.problemStatement:
You are given a positive integer MMM and an array A (1≤Ai≤M)A \ (1 \leq A_i \leq M)A (1≤Ai​≤M) consisting of NNN positive integers.

Construct an array BBB of length NNN such that:


1≤Bi≤M1 \leq B_i \leq M1≤Bi​≤M for all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N;
For all 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N, there does not exist an index 1≤j≤i1 \leq j \leq i1≤j≤i such that the frequency of BjB_jBj​ is more than that of AiA_iAi​ in the subarray [B1,B2,…,Bi][B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_i][B1​,B2​,…,Bi​]
It can be proved that at least one such array BBB exists for any array AAA.

If there are multiple valid arrays, you can print any one.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space separated integers NNN and MMM — the length of the array AAA and upper bound on AiA_iAi​.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers representing a valid array BBB.


It can be proved that at least one such array BBB exists for any array AAA.

If there are multiple valid arrays, you can print any one.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line has two integers N and M. The second line contains N integers A1, A2, ..., AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ M).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N integers B1, B2, ..., BN such that 1 ≤ Bi ≤ M and for every prefix i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) the frequency of each value in B[1..i] does not exceed its frequency in A[1..i]. Any valid array may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we simply copy array A to B. The copied array obviously satisfies the required prefix‑frequency condition.

solution.approach:
The simplest way to satisfy the prefix‑frequency condition is to copy the given array A to B. For every i, the multiset of values in the prefix B[1..i] is exactly the same as in A[1..i], therefore no value appears more often in B than in A. This construction works for any valid input and runs in linear time.
Algorithm:
1. Read T.
2. Loop over test cases:
   a. Read N and M (M is not needed for the construction).
   b. Read the array A of length N.
   c. Output the elements of A in the same order as B.
The algorithm uses O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space besides the input array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Σ N) – each element is read and printed once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the input array of a test case (can be O(1) if streamed).
